title: Bengali Fish Curry

overview.description: A light Bengali fish curry with turmeric-coated fish, potatoes and a fragrant tomato and cumin gravy.

ingredients: 600g firm white fish fillets, such as cod, haddock or pollock
3 tbsp mustard oil, or neutral oil
2 medium potatoes, peeled and cut into large chunks
method: Cut the fish into large pieces.
Season the fish with ½ tsp turmeric and ½ tsp salt.
Heat 2 tbsp of the oil in a large frying pan.
Lightly fry the fish pieces on both sides until lightly coloured but not fully cooked.
Remove the fish and set aside.
Add the remaining oil to the pan if necessary.
Add the potatoes and fry until lightly golden on the outside.
Add the cumin seeds to the pan.
Add the sliced onion and cook until softened.
Add the tomatoes, ground cumin, ground coriander, remaining turmeric and remaining salt.
Cook until the tomatoes soften and the oil begins to separate from the mixture.
Cover and simmer until the potatoes are almost tender.
Add the fish and green chilli if using.
Simmer gently until the fish is cooked through and the gravy has taken on its flavour.
Scatter with fresh coriander and serve.

learning.did-you-know: Fish is central to Bengali cuisine, and the phrase 'machhe bhate Bangali' expresses the cultural importance of fish and rice to Bengali food traditions.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to lightly fry fish before adding it to a curry, build a simple spiced gravy and cook fish gently so it remains intact.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A regional Bengali fish dish that introduces a lighter, thinner style of curry and shows how fish can be the central ingredient rather than simply being added to a generic curry sauce.
chapman-tips: Use firm fish that will hold together during cooking.
Don't fully cook the fish during the initial frying; it will finish cooking in the gravy.
Mustard oil gives the most characteristic Bengali flavour, although neutral oil works well if you don't have it.
If using mustard oil, heat it until it is very hot and just beginning to smoke, then allow it to cool slightly before cooking.
Keep the curry at a gentle simmer once the fish is added.
Avoid stirring aggressively; gently shake the pan instead to avoid breaking up the fish.
The gravy should be relatively thin rather than thick and creamy.
notes.batch-cooking: Best eaten fresh. The curry can be refrigerated and reheated gently, but the fish may become softer. Freezing is possible but the texture of the fish may suffer.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with fresh rice. Flaked leftover fish can also be used in a fish wrap or mixed into a rice dish.
